sentences.text: Mm-hmm.
Scared.
They saw me coming in with a gun.
They were there, yes.
They didn't know anything about it.
They lied straight into your face.
The daughter didn't say a word.
The last name, Bauer.
That's, that's what I remember because I saw it on the door.
I was there for about 10 minutes.
Mm-hmm.
Nothing.
Like, they would expect something to happen to them.
You mean kill the woman and the daughter?
I didn't think I had the heart or anything like this.
Because I was so full of hatred, I never thought of a woman or a daughter would be-- that I could do something like this.
No.
Just went, and we went on looking for more.
I-- we found very few men in any house that we went into.
And we saw somebody, like, we thought he is too old.
That he couldn't have done anything, maybe he was not even involved in anything.
But Dachau, the whole town looked like-- like the whole town would be full of Nazis.
You know, you could tell that the whole town is military-like.
Dachau was not the big city.
It was a small town.
Mm-hmm.
That was, like-- we were traveling the whole day in cattle trains.
We were-- we knew-- they said the we were going to Dachau.
I heard of Dachau, yes.
We heard of Dachau.
We heard from all-- a lot of camps.
We didn't hear anything about Auschwitz or Birkenau because we were away hundreds and hundreds of miles.
You know, Auschwitz is in Poland, and Dachau is near Munich.
The first procedure that I arrived in Dachau, when they let us off from the trains, and we had to march in towards the camp.
When we got through the gates, like I told you, we saw-- you looked around.
You see bodies laying near a big building and it stunk.
You see the smoke coming out of those big chimneys.
So you know that it's a factor where they burn people there.
Terrible.
It smelled really, really bad.
It smelled like bones when you burn bones.
I don't know if you ever-- from a chicken even.
Or rags, when you burn, it stinks.
And so it stunk.
And they had tables from the main gate, maybe the distance, like, from the door over there to the step over there, all lines of tables across with the different initials from names.
Like, you know, that the-- like a little marker that says A, B, C, and so on.
So everybody should know where to go, to what table.
Mm-hmm.
So people start going, lined up in lines towards the tables.
And me, we didn't have any documents or any papers on us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But when they said-- when we came they said, Jews to stay where you are.
All different nationalities should go out.
So the Jews had to-- you know, they had stay in that line by themselves.
And I thought to myself, either way I'm going to be dead tomorrow if they're going to find out that I'm a Jew.
I'm going to be tomorrow in the crematorium.
So I took a chance.
I didn't stay with the Jews.
I walked into the other lines.
With non-Jews.
Dachau had all different nationalities.
Dachau had even Germans in there.
And you could recognize them by the uniforms with the numbers.
Each nationality had their own marker before the number.
Like, the Jew would have a yellow star.
Poland would have white and blue.
German would have green, something else with it, you know.
Before the number.
A mark before the number.
You know, you saw the mark, so you could recognize the nationality of the prisoner.
And then the number would be the number of the prisoner.
There was no name.
If they called you, they called you 8467 or whatever, that you knew that that was you.
So when I walked out towards the place with the Gentiles, I came over to the table where I was next.
But meanwhile, I had my mind already made up, what name I should use.
So I remembered another Polish boy that went to school together with me.
His name was Baransky, Stanisla.
It just, just came in to me, and that's what I used.
When they asked me the name, I told them Stanisla Baransky.
And I was lucky by it because the next day I heard that the Jewish people, they were already finished.
They were sent to the gas chamber and to burn at the crematorium after.
The Jewish, yeah.
But still by the liberation, after the-- after the liberation, I was able to find a few Jewish prisoners that did the same thing that I did.
And some of them were there in Dachau, that they brought them in a day before from another camp.
That they had to fill up the quota there.

But a lot of them, towards the end-- after the liberation, a lot of friends of mine that became friends, Jewish and non-Jewish, they died anyway because when the Americans came in, they were trying to be too good.
They were trying to give whatever they had to eat, and some of it was so, so rich that the prisoners died of from it, after they didn't eat for so many years.
And here they gave them milk and chocolate and all that other stuff that their stomachs couldn't take it and they died.
The other nationalities there were mainly working inside the camp, which it was not too hard work.
So they just had it so much easier.
They had different kitchens.
They gave them better food.
They treated them different than they treated us-- completely different.
They had-- they call it-- I forgot what they call this-- like, a PX in camp.
They would get, every week, coupons to go into the PX.
And for whatever the coupon they had, they could go in, and pick out whatever they wanted.
We never had that, the Jewish people.
Yes.
They took us, every day, to Munich.
Most of it to Munich to clean up after the planes bombarded the night before.
The planes come in-- came in even during the day, a lot of times, to bombard the city.
The city was such a mess that-- Munich is a big city.
It's almost like-- compares to, you know, to New York.
People couldn't find their way into the houses.
That's how they were smashed.
But at that time, it was like a holiday for us because no matter what, you could find some food.
Either if it-- it was a dead dog or anything.
It was food.
We had-- we could find something to eat.
Sometimes, in a bombarded house, you could find some bread, or find a potato.
So it was good for us.
I couldn't tell you because we hardly would get in contact with anybody around the surrounded area of the bombarding.
And on top of it, we were guarded very, very close.
The German SS, with the dogs, so you couldn't-- you couldn't get close to anybody.
But the biggest fun was when they bombarded some warehouses, where they had food prepared for the Germans to towards the front lines.
There we had the best time.
There we had everything that we wanted.
Even bombarded, we had cookies, chocolates, cigarettes, candies, everything you want to think of.
You know, the magazine-- you know, the warehouses.
That's supposed to be shipped out towards the front line for the front line soldiers.
Gross-Rosen.
Like I told you before, the atrocities that I saw in there with the children's clothes laid in the front of barracks.
That was-- that hit it so hard, terrible, because we knew that those children were either gassed in Gross-Rosen or they were gassed and killed in other camps.
And they brought in all the food-- all the clothes, with all the shoes, with all the glasses-- eyeglasses.
Everything to that camp, and we saw, in the front of the line of the barracks, lined up, such a big, big mountains of it.
That could make-- it made you sick looking at it.
Six years.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From '39 until I was liberated in May 1945.
I-- you know, when I became friends with the soldier that liberated me.
Jerome Klein.
So I stayed in Dachau until he was shipped out to Munich, which it was, I think, two weeks later.
He took me with him.
When we got to Munich, he went through a place, not too far from where he was staying in the-- in the building.
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
It was near Rotkreuzplatz.
Went in-- he found out-- they found out, first, where the German SS live or used to live.
He would go into the building.
I would make him empty the building.
Just take whatever you wear-- an extra pair of pants, an extra pair of shoes-- and leave everything else.
And then he told me to put-- to get in there.
He told the German, give up the keys, you cannot live here anymore.
He did.
Mm-hmm.
I was afraid to be in the building by myself.
I figured there could be somebody there that could come in at night and put a bullet through me.
I got myself a gun, and I slept with it that first night.
Next morning somebody knocks at the door.
It was a woman that used to come in there to clean the house every day.
I did let her in, and I told her here are my shoes.
I want you to shine my shoes.
I want you to make me something to eat for breakfast.
You know, I gave them orders instead they're giving us orders.
She could tell.
She could tell by the accent.
I spoke German, but they could tell.
They knew that there were a lot of prisoners in Munich now because a lot of them came in.
They all did the same thing.
Oh, yes.
She would even go down on the floor.
They were afraid.
Actually, I lived in Munich until I came over to this country.
I'd say about two years.
No work.
We wouldn't go to work for Germans after you come out of concentration camp.
Black marketing.
Anything you could put your hands on.
Even the American Military did black marketing, too.
So you did business.
You know, they gave you something to sell.
You sold it-- coffee, chocolate.
Sometimes, I remember, they'd bring you ham-- whole hams wrapped up in, like, burlap, and you tried to sell it.
And you buy for the money whatever you want.
But to go to work for a German after being six years in a concentration camp, nobody would ever think of it.
Yes.
I met my wife in a DP-- a displaced persons camp, which it was 300 kilometers from Munich.
I went there because I knew I would find a lot of people that I knew.
Yes.
You know, after the liberation, you know, from all different camps, they would go out to DP camps.
They had no place to stay.
A lot of people arrived from being with the underground in Russia.
So they had to go to a displaced persons camp because they had no other place to go to.
And the joint distribution there would take care of them.
So there was food from that, and if there were any children born, or little children, everything was taken care of.
Nope.
No.
No reason.
No.
I had-- yes, because I met Jerome.
We became so friendly.
Then, after he was shipped home from Germany, first thing, he wrote me a letter to ask me if I would like to come over to the United States.
I told him-- I said now he's making a joke because I have no relative in this country.
Somebody has to sponsor you.
Send him back a letter.
And he says, that's no joke.
My parents were so happy to see me coming home alive from the war.
I was the only son.
They decided that if you want to come to the United States, they're going to make all the necessary papers for you, which they did.
And they send me the papers.
I went over with the papers to the HIAS in Germany, showed it to them, and says well, if you want to go to the United States, you're going to have to go through the consulate.
You're going to have to go through medical things, and all that.
So I sent back and I says, yes, I would like to come to the United States.
But I think it's a joke that you're playing on me because there's no way for me to come to the United States.
Worked out that it was no joke.
Now, when you came here, did you come with the woman who eventually became your wife or did you come alone?
No.
No.
No.
She came over two years later on the same ship, which I have the document--
Yeah.
It was an old converted army ship.
It took us 11 days to cross the ocean.
And that-- when I arrived in New York, the whole [NON-ENGLISH], you know, friends, everybody, they were waiting on the dock.
They didn't know for who to look at, but they had a picture of me.
So the--
His family.
And all their friends with Jerome.
They took me off the ship right away after I was going through customs and all that.
They took me to their house.
They lived in Brooklyn, on Utica Avenue.
And for three months they wouldn't let me do a thing.
They wouldn't let me go look for any work or anything.
Just to have me-- give me, everyday, different party with their friends, with their families.
Everybody would bring presents and everybody was looking for somebody, you know, to introduce you to somebody, you know.
And right away a lot of woman came over and they says, oh, I have a daughter for you.
And I says, I'm sorry, I have a girlfriend.
And she may come over pretty soon.
And so soon I made enough money after I took a job.
In a factory.
Soon, I had made enough money, I sent for my wife.
She was my girlfriend, and she came over a year later.
And after she was here awhile, we got married here.
I didn't like New York.
I was not used to a big city, didn't know the language.
So I found out about a friend of mine who was in concentration camp with me together, that he lives in New Haven.
So I called him up.
And I says that I am in New York.
He invited me to come down.
I come down on Sunday, get off the station, get out from the station, and I see that's more of a town that I would like-- that I'm used to.
I went back the next day, Monday, took my suitcase, came down to New Haven.
When?
Well, I was working from the beginning in the tailor shop.
Then, after that, I opened up my own shop.
In New Haven.
And I am very-- well, I mean, anybody in the street-- doesn't matter black, white, policemen, anybody-- you ask for Sidney, everybody knows me.
